Microelectronics Journal  
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/mejo  
Statistical gate-delay modeling with copulas  
Walter Schneider  
HAW Landshut, University of Applied Sciences, Landshut, Germany  
A R T I C L E I N F O  
A B S T R A C T  
Keywords:  
The growing impact of process variations on circuit performance has become a major concern for deep-submicron  
integrated circuit design, resulting in numerous SSTA-algorithms. The acceptance of such algorithms in industry  
however will be dependent on modeling the real silicon behavior in SSTA. This includes that the statistical  
gate-delay models must consider arbitrary process variations and dependencies. In this paper, we introduce the  
innovative concept of Copulas to handle this topic. A complete Matlab based framework starting from process  
parameter statistics up to the computation of the statistical gate-delay distribution is presented. Experimental  
results demonstrate the importance of accounting realistic process variations.  
Statistical gate-delay  
Process variations  
Copulas  
1. Introduction  
1. Ignoring variations of slope and load  
2. Reduction of the amount of varying process parameters, mostly  
In CMOS digital design, accurate timing characterization of logic  
gates and digital circuits is essential. For Static Timing Analysis (STA)  
it is common practice that two dimensional lookup-tables are used for  
describing the delay of cells dependent on input slope and load capaci-  
tance. By technology downscaling deep into the nanometer era, process  
variations increase signiﬁcantly and will cause high variability in circuit  
performance. The photolithography process is one important source of  
process variations which impacts the channel length L and the chan-  
nel width W of a MOS transistor. Another well known example is the  
random dopant ﬂuctuation (RDF) which aﬀects the transistor thresh-  
old voltage. The traditional way to treat variations is to consider pro-  
cess corners at which a logic cell has its worst-case delay. However, for  
an increasing number of process variations this corner-based approach  
using STA becomes computationally expensive. Furthermore, with a  
assumed to be normally distributed  
3. Limitation of the parameters’ dependencies by means of a simple  
linear correlation coeﬃcient.  
Despite of all enhancements concerning these issues (e.g. addressing  
non-normal distributions in [1–3]) a common view on the complete pro-  
cess parameter statistics given from a semiconductor manufacturer and  
its translation on a statistical gate-delay model is still missing. The most  
promising approach in this context is the research paper [4], where the  
authors have introduced the Generalized Lambda Distribution (GLD)  
together with rank correlations. Unfortunately, the presented results do  
not show the actual process statistics and their correlations. Further-  
more, Monte Carlo runs are necessary to get rank correlation coeﬃ-  
cients as well as the GLD parameters.  
Additionally, the high computational cost of SSTA algorithms turned  
out as a stumbling block for practical usage leading to several on-chip  
variation (OCV) approaches in recent years. In parametric on-chip vari-  
ation (POCV) each cell is characterized by a delay variation ꢀ obtained  
through Monte-Carlo SPICE simulation [5]. Using the Liberty Variation  
Format (LVF) which reports cell-delay variation for diﬀerent slope/load  
combinations improves the accuracy for 16 nm designs and below [6,7].  
Cadence Liberate Trio Characterization Suite generates LVF libraries using  
statistical SPICE models [8] - however problem (3) from above has not  
exhaustively been solved.  
ꢀ
ꢁ
decrease in the transistor size the -ratio of the varying parameters  
proportionally increase and leads to overly pessimistic and suboptimal  
designs.  
Statistical Static Timing Analysis (SSTA) has been proposed as an  
improvement since the actual statistics of the process parameters are  
considered via random variables (RV). In general, the SSTA algorithms  
can be classiﬁed into two categories: the block-based and the path-  
based algorithms. Although many research papers addressing both tech-  
niques have been published in the past it must be asserted, that SSTA  
is still not widely utilized in industry. One main reason is due to some  
simpliﬁed assumptions for statistical gate-delay modeling, such as:  
In this paper, the principal idea of [4] is extended in two essential  
aspects: 1) instead of the Generalized Lambda Distribution any distribu-  
tion function (e.g. Lognormal, Gamma, Weibull, ExtremeValue …) can

especially the channel length as the smaller CD of a transistor. Depen-  
dent on lithography technologies the 3ꢀ variation in the amplitude of  
LER can vary between 4 nm and 8 nm for a line width of 80 nm [11].  
The impact of LER also includes variation in Vth and is given by [12,13]:  
be used for a process parameter and 2) instead of the rank correlations  
we use the theory of Copulas, which allows a more ﬂexible modeling of  
parameter dependencies and therefore seems to be also interesting for  
the generation of LVF libraries. Using these two facts we are convinced  
that the statistical gate-delay model will really reﬂect the true silicon  
behavior.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In the next Section  
we give a short overview on the variability of process parameters. In  
Section 3 we review the basic principles of gate-delay modeling. Then  
in Section 4 the theoretical background of Copulas is introduced. In  
Section 5 we present our statistical gate-delay framework based on Mat-  
lab and its associated Statistics Toolbox, which also incorporates a com-  
fortable Copula package. Experimental results are discussed in Section  
6 and ﬁnally we conclude in Section 7.  
As it is expected that LER is continue rising in future technologies,  
variations in L, W and Vth also will increase.  
2.1.3. Oxide thickness variation (OTV)  
OTV is caused by atomic scale roughness between silicon and gate  
dielectric [13]. In 45 nm technologies and beyond, OTV is becoming  
more dominant as Tox approaches to less than 2 nm. OTV introduces  
variations of the gate current Ig which leads to variations in Vth. Also  
the electron mobility ꢁ is inﬂuenced by OTV.  
2. Process variations  
Among the discussed sources of process variability and others (e.g.  
Optical Proximity Eﬀect (OPE) or carrier velocity saturation with  
impact on channel length and electron mobility) LER and RDF have  
attracted most attention because it is expected that variations in L and  
Vth have the most impact on performance variation.  
Amongst a huge number of parameters, which aﬀect the perfor-  
mance behavior of MOSFET devices, the geometry parameters channel  
length (L), channel width (W) as well as the physical parameters oxide  
thickness (Tox), electron mobility (ꢁ) and threshold voltage (Vth) are the  
most important [9]. We brieﬂy outline the major sources of variation of  
these parameters.  
2.2. Statistics of process variations  
Varying process parameters are usually treated as RVs. The assump-  
tion about normally distributed parameters is very convenient for SSTA.  
However this does not always reﬂect the true behavior. It is well known  
that some parameters really diﬀer from a normal distribution: e. g.  
channel length or via resistances show an asymmetric non-Gaussian dis-  
tribution [14,15]. Also the number of channel dopants Na is observed to  
be well modeled by a Poisson distribution aﬀecting the distribution of  
the threshold voltage Vth, which is diﬀerent from a normal distribution.  
This is illustrated in Fig. 2, where the Weibull distribution seems to be  
an adequate ﬁt for the variability of Vth.  
2.1. Sources of process variations  
2.1.1. Random dopant ﬂuctuation (RDF)  
RDF is caused by the random placement of the dopant atoms in the  
channel region of a transistor. With shrinking technologies the total  
number of dopant atoms decreases which results in an increase of the  
threshold voltage variation as shown in Fig. 1.  
The standard deviation of threshold voltage variation due to RDF is  
given by  
The most diﬃculty is to get precise realistic process statistics from  
the industry. These statistics should contain the complete distribution  
and not only the ﬁrst two moments ꢁ and ꢀ. Moreover the knowl-  
edge about the dependency amongst the diﬀerent process parameters  
is quite essential, meaning that the speciﬁcation of a simple linear cor-  
relation coeﬃcient is not suﬃcient for the accurate description of the  
dependency between RVs. To illustrate this fact we consider two RVs  
X, Y ∼ N(0, 1), which are correlated with ꢃ(X, Y) = 0.85. The structure  
of the data points (x, y) can be quite diﬀerent as shown in Fig. 3: the  
left part shows a strong upper tail dependency, the right part however  
a strong lower tail dependency despite of the same ꢃ.

2.1.2. Line edge roughness (LER)  
LER results from sub-wavelength lithography and etching process  
that causes variation in the critical dimensions (CD) of a transistor,

Fig. 5. Example of a lookup-table.  
dent on slope/load conditions. Therefore, two dimensional lookup-  
tables are built for each timing arc from input to output. This means  
that, for the inverter above, two lookup-tables (for rising and falling  
input) would be necessary. The drawback of this approach is with  
respect to varying parameters, since the delay dependency from pro-  
cess parameters is hidden. The only solution is given by introduc-  
ing additional tables for best-/worst-case conditions, which are help-  
ful for corner analysis. This is illustrated by an example lookup-table  
(NC = normal-case, BC = best-case, WC = worst-case) in Fig. 5 for one  
speciﬁc timing arc.  
Fig. 3. Diﬀerent dependencies of two identical distributed RVs with the same  
linear correlation coeﬃcient.  
Analytical Equations. Analytical gate-delay modeling has been the  
subject of much research, especially for a basic inverter. Early models  
were based on Shockley’s MOSFET model. Later on the alpha-power  
law MOSFET model considering the carrier velocity saturation eﬀect  
of short channel devices was introduced by Sakurai [16]. Hence the  
inverter delay td can be written as  
Tox  
CLVdd

td =

(4)

W
(Vdd − Vth)ꢄ  
where ꢄ is the Sakurai index and can be taken equal to 1 in scaled  
technologies. The authors in [17] extended this model to account  
for input slope and complex gates by conversion into an equivalent  
inverter. Despite of further improvement of analytical models account-  
ing for physical eﬀects (e.g. I/O coupling, channel-length modula-  
tion, drain-induced barrier lowering DIBL) [18–21], deriving a compact  
closed delay formula for a wide range of slope/load conditions remains  
challenging.  
Fig. 4. Inverter-delay for a rising input ramp.  
From this illustrative example it becomes clear, that a deeper insight  
into the dependency structure between RVs is necessary. Otherwise  
the statistical gate-delay models and the SSTA algorithms will not be  
trustable.  
Compared to the lookup-tables the analytical model has the advan-  
tage, that the dependency on process parameters is explicit known  
which is very helpful for the statistical analysis.  
Polynomial Parametric Delay Models (PDM). The majority of SSTA  
algorithms use polynomial parametric delay models with a polynomial  
of degree r. In case of r = 2 we get the popular quadratic timing model  
Q (e.g. used in Ref. [15]) as  
3. Overview on gate-delay modeling  
The delay of a logic gate is deﬁned as the time interval Δt between  
the 50% levels of input and output signal. Clearly Δt depends on pro-  
cess parameters ⃖p⃗ (e.g. L, Vth, …as discussed in the previous Section)  
as well as external factors given by supply voltage Vdd, temperature T,  
load capacitance CL and input slope sin. Thus in general the gate-delay  
td(=̂Δt) can be written as

square solution of:

deﬁnition for an inverter and a rising input ramp, whereat the drain  
current IDn discharges the capacitance CL via the nMOS transistor.  
Before looking at the statistical behavior of td, we give a short  
overview about diﬀerent methods to determine the nominal gate-delay.

This makes clear that a strategy is necessary to deal with diﬀerent  
dependency structures and of course with arbitrary process parameter  
distributions. For this reason now we introduce the concept of Copulas.  
4. Dependency modeling using copulas  
Copulas are helpful in the understanding of dependency at a deeper  
level meaning that the dependency is not restricted to the simple case  
of linear correlation. Copulas combine arbitrary marginal distribution  
functions (df) to a joint distribution function with a variety of possible  
dependency models, which are isolated from the marginals. From these  
dependency models rank correlations as in [4] or coeﬃcients of tail  
dependency can be derived. In the following the most important basic  
properties of copulas are summarized. The reader is referred to [26] for  
further detailled information.  
Deﬁnition 4.1. A d-dimensional copula C is a df on the unit hyper-  
cube [0, 1]d with standard uniform marginal distributions, C ∶ [0, 1]d  
→
[0, 1].

1. Quantile transformation. If U ∼ U(0, 1) has a standard uniform dis-

3.2. Statistical gate-delay characterization  
2. Probability transformation. If Y has df G, where G is a continuous  
univariate df, then G(Y) ∼ U(0, 1).  
Dependent on the chosen nominal gate-delay model, in general there  
are two possibilities to obtain its probability density function (pdf) or  
cumulative distribution function (cdf):  
The construction of multivariate dfs with univariate dfs by using  
copulas is stated in Sklar’s Theorem.  
1. Using familiar rules from mathematical probability theory in case of  
an analytical or polynomial parametric model: e.g. sum/product of  
RVs or the multivariate density transformation theorem [22,23].  
2. Using Monte Carlo (MC) sampling, which is possible for all delay  
models and serves as “Golden reference” for SSTA algorithms. The  
only drawback of this method is the computational cost; there-  
fore many authors have focussed on accelerating the MC runtime  
[24,25].  
Proposition 4.3. (Sklar’s Theorem) Let F be a joint distribution function  
with margins F1, F2, … , Fd. Then there exists a copula C such that, for all  
x1, x2, … , xd ∈ ℝ,  
F(x1, x2, … , xd) = C(F1(x1), F2(x2), … , Fd(xd))  
(7)  
Conversely, if C is a copula and F1, … , Fd are univariate dfs, then  
the function F in (7) is a joint df with the margins F1, … , Fd.  
No matter what delay model is used, the main unresolved problem  
is the consideration of arbitrary process variations and dependencies.  
The following example should highlight this aspect.  
4.1. Example of copulas  
In general copulas can be split into three categories: 1) fundamen-  
tal copulas represent a number of important special dependency struc-  
tures (e.g. independence or perfectly positive (negative) dependency);  
2) implicit copulas are extracted from well-known multivariate distribu-  
tions with Sklar’s Theorem (e.g. Gauss-Copula); 3) explicit copulas with  
simple closed-form expressions (e.g. Archimedian copula family). Some  
of them should be discussed for the bivariate case in more detail.  
Gauss-Copula. For a bivariate normal (Gaussian) RV X its copula is

From the left part (the histogram for the case with lower tail depen-  
dency) it can be seen that the inverter-delay distribution signiﬁcantly  
diﬀers from a normal distribution. Obviously for both dependencies the  
principal empirical CDFs (derived from the histogram) look very simi-  
lar. But in the interesting region between the 0.90 and 0.99 quantiles  
we see that the lower tail dependency is more conservative. This results  
in a diﬀerence of roughly Δt = 6ps (≈ 12% in relation to ꢁ = 49ps)  
for the 0.975 quantile.

By using the algorithms from above for each case 5000 data pairs  
(X, Y) have been generated. Fig. 7 shows the result: despite of same

the Gumbel- Copala shows an upper tail dependency, whereas for the  
t-Copula there is both an upper and lower tail dependency.

4.2. Simulation of copulas  
One primary application of copulas is in simulation, in which the  
problem of generating samples from a given copula must be addressed.  
In case of explicit copulas this is achieved by Algorithm 1.  
Algorithm 3 Generation of SampleMatrix P.

(X, Y) with margins FX, FY and explicit 2-dim. copula C

6. Experimental results  
Our approach for statistical gate-delay modeling has been imple-  
mented in Matlab and tested using an inverter in 45 nm CMOS tech-  
nology. First, a sensitivity SPICE analysis conﬁrms that the discussed  
parameters in Section 2: L, W, Vth, Tox and VMAX (for modeling the  
decrease in mobility ꢁ due to velocity saturation) are crucial for the  
inverter-delay td. These parameters together with load capacitance CL  
and input slope sin form the parameter vector ⃖p⃗ .  
6.1. Quality of the nominal PDM  
For the polynomial parametric delay model (PDM), each component  
of ⃖p⃗ is expressed as a triple: pi = [pi , pi , pi ] where these values are  
given from the 45 nm Predictive TechnologymMaxodel (PTM) [29]. CL and  
sin are set to [10 fF, 30 fF, 50 fF] and [2ps, 5ps, 10ps] respectively.  
A SPICE simulation of all parameter constellations yields 37 = 2187  
delay values td. The Matlab algorithm polyﬁtn [30] reports the quality  
of a ﬁtting polynomial with degree r. It can be seen, that a quadratic  
model (including cross terms like p1 · p2) yields an accurate result with  
an average error of less than 4%. The linear or “pure” quadratic (with-  
out cross terms) model however are much worse as shown in Fig. 9.  
So, the quadratic delay model will be used for comparing our diﬀerent  
statistical modeling approaches.  
min nom  
Fig. 9. Comparison between diﬀerent PDM models.  
Matlab, e.g.: beta, gamma, generalized extreme value, weibull,  
…amongst others.  
∙ The generated matrix P can also be used in combination with a  
Monte-Carlo SPICE simulation.  
Certainly, getting realistic parameter distributions and dependen-  
cies from a semiconductor company is challenging. Nevertheless, Algo-  
rithm 3 can also be applied even if only some roughly information  
concerning distributions and dependencies is available: in this case the  
SampleMatrix P is generated with diﬀerent copulas and margins and  
the eﬀect on the timing behavior of td is analyzed. So, bounds for a  
speciﬁc ith-quantile of td are predicted.  
6.2. Delay distribution with diﬀerent parameter statistics  
Now we want to study the impact of various distributions of p⃖⃗ on  
the CDF of td.

(nN), we consider the following distributions:

Taking slope and load as RVs is justiﬁed by the fact that treating them as

Microelectronics Journal 107 (2021) 104938  
Fig. 12. Histograms and Dependencies of normal and non-normal (nN) dis-  
tributed parameters with Gauss-Copula CGa and ꢃ = 0.8.  
- CL, sin ∼ N(ꢁ, ꢀ), - VMAX ∼ LN(ꢁ, ꢀ)  
- Vth ∼ W(a, b), - Tox ∼ EV(ꢁ)  
- W ∼ GEV(ꢁ, ꢀ, k), - L ∼ G(a, b).  
with N as normal, LN as lognormal, G as Gamma, EV as ExtremeValue,  
GEV as GeneralizedExtremeValue and W as Weibull distribution. By trans-  
ferring the normally distributed parameters from A) to the desired dis-  
tribution, we obtain the relevant parameters ꢁ, ꢀ as well as a, b, k for  
each case. For this, the Distribution Fitter App dﬁttool from Matlab is  
very helpful [28]. The principal shape of the parameter distributions  
is shown by the histograms in Fig. 12, where the dependency of the  
parameters is according to a Gauss-Copula CGa and ꢃ = 0.8.  
As in the case of normally distributed parameters (N) also for a  
mixture of normally and non-normally distributed parameters (nN) the  
numerical values of the 0.99-quantiles reveal, that the dependency  
structure is important: e.g. for testcase ♯12 in Table 1 the q99-value  
with t-copula is more than 5ps larger than the corresponding value with  
Gauss-Copula, an increase of 11.8%.  
Fig. 13. Eﬀect of diﬀerent inverter delay distributions on the path delay distri-  
bution of an inverter chain.  
6.2.3. Archimedian Copulas as dependency model  
Finally, we examine Archimedian Copulas, where we take the  
Clayton- and Gumbel-Copula as introduced in Section 4. We adopt the  
non-normal parameter settings from B) but instead of a dependency  
between all parameters only a pairwise dependency is applied (this is  
only for practical reason as the Matlab function copularnd is limited  
to 2-dim. Archimedian copulas). Fig. 11 shows the dependency for CCl  
(Clayton) and CGu (Gumbel) with lower and upper tail dependency as  
outlined in Section 4. The ꢇ parameter for both copulas has been cho-  
sen, such that in both cases the same linear correlation ꢃ = 0.8 is  
realized.2  
delays) ﬁnished in less than 2 s. Considering some additional time eﬀort  
for the nominal delay computation, a 20-30x speed up is achieved.  
6.3. Application on inverter-chain  
In a ﬁnal experiment with a 10-stage inverter chain we want to  
analyze the eﬀect of diﬀerent single inverter delay distributions on the  
resulting path delay distribution (Fig. 13). In doing so we use the results  
of testcase ♯8 from Table 1 and consider the following two scenarios for  
the distribution of the inverter delay td:  
Though the resulting CDFs of the inverter-delay td according to CCl  
and CGu are very similar, an appreciable diﬀerence in the higher quan-  
tiles exists. From Fig. 11 we extract the 0.99-quantiles as 41.1 ps for CCl  
∙ td-distribution as the result of normally distributed parame-  
ters with Gaussian Copula CGa (column 7 in Table 1 with  
q99 = 47.8ps)  
and 38.4 ps for CGu. With respect to ꢁt = 20ps this diﬀerence of 2.7ps  
d
implies a discrepancy of 13.5%.  
While Monte-Carlo SPICE simulations are very time consuming this  
is diﬀerent to our Matlab computation. SPICE simulations took more  
than 150 s to complete a single inverter delay analysis with 10000 runs.  
The Matlab algorithm (copula generation + computation of 10000 gate  
∙ td-distribution as the result of normally distributed parameters  
with t-Copula Ct (column 9 in Table 1 with q99 = 50.2ps)  
From each of these 2 distributions 10 independent samples for the  
10 inverter delays td1, … , td10 with a sample size of 10000 for each  
delay are generated. The addition of the inverter delays yields the path  
delay tpath = td1 + · · · + td10 for CGa and Ct, which in turn is a tuple of  
10000 delay values each. Using these values Matlab’s cdfplot automati-  
2
The Matlab function copulaparam automatically computes ꢇ by using the  
linear correlation ꢃ or the rank correlation ꢉ.  
9
     
W. Schneider  
Microelectronics Journal 107 (2021) 104938  
cally creates the corresponding CDF’s, which are illustrated in Fig. 13.  
Obviously there is a remarkable diﬀerence between the two curves, e.g.  
the discrepancy of the 0.99-quantile delay is about 10%. It turned out,  
that this diﬀerence could be even worse - e.g. more than 20% for test-  
case ♯3. It is expected that such signiﬁcant diﬀerences appear in a simi-  
lar manner when regarding some arbitrary paths with various complex  
gates in a design.

no. 9.

7. Conclusion  
[13] S.K. Saha, Compact MOSFET modeling for process variability-aware VLSI circuit  
design, in: IEEE Access, 2014.  
[14] H. Chang, V. Zolotov, S. Narayan, C. Visweswariah, Parameterized block-based  
statistical timing analysis with non-Gaussian parameters, nonlinear delay  
functions, in: Proc. of DAC, 2005.  
SSTA is an important topic in the design of integrated circuits espe-  
cially in the nanometer regime. The quality of SSTA algorithms strongly  
depends on the underlying statistical gate-delay models. Only with  
accurate models it is expected that such algorithms are accepted in  
industry. This paper demonstrates the novel modeling technique Copu-  
las to combine any type of statistical parameter distribution with arbi-  
trary dependencies. The eﬀect of diﬀerent dependencies and parame-  
ter distributions has been extensively studied with a CMOS inverter.  
The reported results have shown signiﬁcant diﬀerences concerning the  
inverter delay distribution and consequently the path delay distribution  
as shown by a simple inverter chain. Future work includes the analysis  
of other cells and the application of copulas to path based SSTA algo-  
rithms.

and arbitrary variation sources, in: Proc. of DAC, 2008.

CMOS inverter delay and other formulas, in: IEEE Journal Solid-State Circuits, vol.  
25, 1990, pp. 584–594. no. 2.

model for ultra-deep-submicrometer CMOS digital design, in: IEEE 22nd  
International Conference on VLSI Design, 2009.  
[18] Y. Wang, M. Zwolinski, Analytical transient response and propagation delay model  
for nanoscale CMOS inverter, in: IEEE International Symposium on Circuits and

to eﬃcient circuit variability analysis in scaled CMOS design, in: 13th  
International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design, 2010.  
[20] F.S. Marranghello, A.I. Reis, R.P. Ribas, Improving analytical delay modeling for  
CMOS inverters, in: Journal of Integrated Circuits and Systems, 2015.  
[21] P. Cao, Z. Liu, J. Guo, J. Wu, An analytical gate delay model in near/subthreshold  
domain considering process variation, in: IEEE Access, vol. 7, 2019.
